content:
# Exodus Chapter 14 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~40v-41r (PDF pp. 76-77); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 54-55 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_014_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_page_002.jpg`

---

## YHWH Sets the Trap (vv. 1-4)

**14:1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: [fn1]

**14:2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, and let them turn back and encamp before Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, before Baal-zephon -- opposite it you shall encamp by the sea. [fn2]

**14:3** And Pharaoh will say concerning the sons of Israel, 'They are wandering aimlessly in the land; the wilderness has closed in on them.' [fn3]

**14:4** And I will strengthen Pharaoh's heart, and he will pursue after them. And I will be glorified through Pharaoh and through all his army, and the Egyptians will know that I am YHWH." And they did so. [fn4]

---

## Pharaoh Pursues Israel (vv. 5-9)

**14:5** And it was told to the king of Egypt that the people had fled. And the heart of Pharaoh and his servants was turned against the people, and they said, "What is this we have done, that we have sent Israel away from serving us?" [fn5]

**14:6** And he harnessed his chariot, and he took his people with him -- [fn6]

**14:7** six hundred chosen chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and officers over all of them. [fn7]

**14:8** And YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh, king of Egypt, and he pursued after the sons of Israel. And the sons of Israel were going out with a high hand. [fn8]

**14:9** And the Egyptians pursued after them and overtook them encamping by the sea -- all the horses and chariots of Pharaoh, and his horsemen, and his army -- by Pi-hahiroth, before Baal-zephon. [fn9]

---

## Israel's Terror and Moses' Response (vv. 10-14)

**14:10** And Pharaoh drew near, and the sons of Israel lifted up their eyes, and behold -- Egypt was marching after them; and they feared greatly, and the sons of Israel cried out to YHWH. [fn10]

**14:11** And they said to Moses, "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt that you have taken us to die in the wilderness? What is this you have done to us, bringing us out from Egypt? [fn11]

**14:12** Is this not the word that we spoke to you in Egypt, saying, 'Leave us alone and let us serve Egypt'? For it would have been better for us to serve Egypt than to die in the wilderness." [fn12]

**14:13** And Moses said to the people, "Do not fear. Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH which he will do for you today; for the Egyptians whom you see today, you will never see them again, unto eternity. [fn13]

**14:14** YHWH will fight for you, and you shall be silent." [fn14]

---

## YHWH's Command to Move Forward (vv. 15-18)

**14:15** And YHWH said to Moses, "Why do you cry out to me? Speak to the sons of Israel, and let them journey on. [fn15]

**14:16** And you -- lift up your staff and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it, and the sons of Israel shall go into the midst of the sea on dry ground. [fn16]

**14:17** And I -- behold, I am strengthening the heart of the Egyptians, and they will come in after them. And I will be glorified through Pharaoh and through all his army, through his chariots and through his horsemen. [fn17]

**14:18** And the Egyptians will know that I am YHWH, when I am glorified through Pharaoh, through his chariots, and through his horsemen." [fn18]

---

## The Angel and the Pillar Move (vv. 19-20)

**14:19** And the angel of God, the one going before the camp of Israel, journeyed and went behind them. And the pillar of cloud journeyed from before them and stood behind them. [fn19]

**14:20** And it came between the camp of Egypt and the camp of Israel. And there was the cloud and the darkness, and it lit up the night; and the one did not come near the other all the night. [fn20]

---

## The Dividing of the Sea (vv. 21-22)

**14:21** And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and YHWH caused the sea to go by a strong east wind all the night, and he made the sea into dry land, and the waters were divided. [fn21]

**14:22** And the sons of Israel went into the midst of the sea on the dry ground, and the waters were a wall to them on their right and on their left. [fn22]

---

## The Egyptians Pursue into the Sea (vv. 23-25)

**14:23** And the Egyptians pursued and came in after them -- all the horses of Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen -- into the midst of the sea. [fn23]

**14:24** And it was in the morning watch, and YHWH looked down upon the camp of Egypt through the pillar of fire and cloud, and he threw the camp of Egypt into confusion. [fn24]

**14:25** And he removed the wheels of their chariots, and he made them drive with heaviness. And Egypt said, "Let me flee from the face of Israel, for YHWH is fighting for them against Egypt!" [fn25]

---

## The Sea Returns (vv. 26-29)

**14:26** And YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the sea, and the waters will return upon Egypt, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen." [fn26]

**14:27** And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the sea returned at the turning of the morning to its normal flow. And the Egyptians were fleeing to meet it, and YHWH shook off the Egyptians into the midst of the sea. [fn27]

**14:28** And the waters returned and covered the chariots and the horsemen -- all the army of Pharaoh that came after them into the sea. Not even one remained among them. [fn28]

**14:29** And the sons of Israel walked on the dry ground in the midst of the sea, and the waters were a wall to them on their right and on their left. [fn29]

---

## The Great Salvation (vv. 30-31)

**14:30** And YHWH saved Israel on that day from the hand of Egypt. And Israel saw Egypt dead upon the shore of the sea. [fn30]

**14:31** **And Israel saw the great hand which YHWH had done against Egypt. And the people feared YHWH, and they believed in YHWH and in Moses his servant.** [fn31]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- Standard divine-speech formula** -- Hebrew: *vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor*. The formula resumes after the itinerary (13:20) and theophanic pillars (13:21-22). All three translators agree on substance. EMET transliterates "Mosheh"; LOGOS uses "the LORD"; PNEUMA drops "And" and "saying." The consensus follows SOPHIA convention: YHWH transliterated, "Moses" in standard English, conjunction "And" preserved, "saying" retained. LXX: *kai elalesen kurios pros Mousēn legōn*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Veyashuvu*: "let them turn back" -- the counter-intuitive command** -- Hebrew: *dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyashuvu veyachanu lifnei Pi haChirot bein Migdol uvein hayyam lifnei Ba'al Tsefon nikhocho tachanu al-hayyam*. The jussive *veyashuvu* (from *shuv*, "to return, turn back") commands Israel to reverse direction -- away from Canaan, back toward Egypt. This is the divine trap: the geography of entrapment (*Pi-hahiroth*, "mouth of the gorges"; *Migdol*, "tower/fortress"; *Ba'al Tsefon*, "Lord of the North," a Canaanite deity shrine) will make Israel appear boxed in. Every direction spells death -- except the direction God will open. EMET renders "sons of Yisra'el" with transliteration and "Pi-ha-Chirot" and "Ba'al Tsefon" with glosses. LOGOS renders "sons of Israel" with "Pi-hahiroth" and "Baal-zephon." PNEUMA renders "Israelites" and drops the construct "between Migdol and between the sea" to "between Migdol and the sea." The consensus follows LOGOS for the place names and SOPHIA convention for "sons of Israel." The doubled "between...and between" of the Hebrew is simplified to "between...and" for English clarity without loss of meaning. LXX renders Pi-hahiroth as *epenanti tēs epauleos* ("opposite the encampment"), interpreting the Hebrew as a descriptive phrase.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Nevukhim hem ba'arets*: "they are wandering aimlessly in the land"** -- Hebrew: *ve'amar Par'oh livnei Yisra'el nevukhim hem ba'arets sagar aleihem hammidbar*. The participle *nevukhim* (from *navakh/bvkh*, "to be confused, wander, be perplexed") is rare, occurring only here, Esther 3:15, and Joel 1:18. It describes disoriented wandering -- Pharaoh's intended reading of Israel's reversal. The verb *sagar* ("to shut, close") portrays the wilderness as a trap snapping shut. The irony is devastating: the trap is real, but it is set for Pharaoh, not Israel. EMET renders "nevukhim [wandering/confused]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "wandering aimlessly." PNEUMA renders "wandering around in confusion." The consensus follows LOGOS: "wandering aimlessly" -- capturing the sense of directionless movement without the explanatory addition "in confusion" (PNEUMA), which is implied. LXX: *planōntai houtoi en tē gē* ("these are wandering in the land").

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Vechizzaqti* and *ve'ikkavdah*: "I will strengthen" and "I will be glorified" -- the hardening-glory nexus** -- Hebrew: *vechizzaqti et-lev Par'oh veradaf achareihem ve'ikkavdah bePar'oh uvkhol-cheilo veyed'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH*. The verb *vechizzaqti* (Piel perfect of *chazaq* with consecutive vav) means "I will strengthen, I will make firm" -- God as explicit agent of the hardening. This is the first of three *chazaq* Piel occurrences in chapter 14 (vv. 4, 8, 17), all with YHWH as agent. The purpose clause is built on the root *k-v-d*: *ve'ikkavdah* (Niphal of *kaved*, "to be heavy, to be glorious") means "I will be glorified" or "I will get glory." The same root that described Pharaoh's "heavy" (*kaved*) heart now becomes the "glory" (*kavod*) of God. The heaviness of Pharaoh's resistance is transmuted into the weight of YHWH's self-revelation. The knowledge formula *veyed'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH* ("and Egypt will know that I am YHWH") states the theological purpose of the entire sea event. EMET renders "I will make strong... I will get glory." LOGOS renders "I will strengthen... I will be glorified." PNEUMA renders "I will harden... I will display my glory." The consensus renders "I will strengthen" (following EMET/LOGOS, preserving the *chazaq* Piel as "strengthen" per convention established in earlier chapters) and "I will be glorified" (following LOGOS, preserving the Niphal passive sense and the *k-v-d* root connection). PNEUMA's "harden" and "display my glory" are interpretively valid but lose the Hebrew root connections that tie the hardening to the glorification. LXX: *sklērunō tēn kardian Pharaō... endoxasthēsomai en Pharaō*.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Varach ha'am*: "the people had fled" -- Pharaoh's perspective** -- Hebrew: *vayyuggad lemelekh Mitsrayim ki varach ha'am vayyehafekh levav Par'oh va'avadav el-ha'am*. The verb *varach* ("to flee") is Pharaoh's reframing of the departure: from Israel's perspective they were sent out (*shillach*); from Pharaoh's, they escaped. The verb *vayyehafekh* (Niphal of *hafakh*, "to turn, overturn") describes a complete heart reversal -- the same verb used for Sodom's overthrow (Gen. 19:25). The phrase *me'ovdenu* ("from serving us") strips away pretense: Israel was a labor force, and Egypt's regret is economic. All three translators agree on the core rendering. EMET transliterates proper names. PNEUMA renders "We let Israel go -- we have lost our slave labor force!" with interpretive expansion. The consensus follows LOGOS: "What is this we have done, that we have sent Israel away from serving us?" -- preserving the Hebrew's own phrasing without PNEUMA's explanatory addition. LXX: *metestraphē hē kardia Pharaō* ("the heart of Pharaoh was turned about").

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Vayyesor*: "he harnessed"** -- Hebrew: *vayyesor et-rikhbo ve'et-ammo laqach immo*. The verb *vayyesor* (Qal of *asar*, "to bind, harness") signals Pharaoh's personal urgency -- the king himself harnesses his war-chariot. All three translators agree. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Bachur* and *shalishim*: "chosen" chariots and "officers"** -- Hebrew: *vayyiqqach shesh-me'ot rekhev bachur vekhol rekhev Mitsrayim veshalishim al-kullo*. The adjective *bachur* ("chosen, select," from *bakhar*, "to choose") marks the six hundred as the elite strike force. The *shalishim* ("officers," from *shalosh*, "three") were chariot officers -- originally the third man in the chariot crew. EMET renders *shalishim* as "shalishim [officers/third-men]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "officers." PNEUMA renders "each one crewed with officers." The consensus follows LOGOS: "officers" -- the standard rendering, with the etymological connection noted here rather than in the text. LXX: *tristatai epi pantōn* ("captains over all").

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Vayechazzeq* and *beyad ramah*: "YHWH strengthened" and "with a high hand"** -- Hebrew: *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitsrayim vayyirdof acharei benei Yisra'el uvenei Yisra'el yots'im beyad ramah*. The second *chazaq* Piel in the chapter: YHWH as narrated agent of hardening. The phrase *beyad ramah* ("with a high/raised hand") is an idiom of boldness, defiance, and visible confidence -- the raised fist of the liberated. The same phrase appears in Num. 15:30 (for deliberate sin) and Num. 33:3 (for the Exodus departure). The contrast with *nevukhim* (v. 3) is deliberate: Israel marches out boldly, but Pharaoh will misread their encampment as confused wandering. EMET renders "be-yad ramah [with a high/uplifted hand]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "with a high hand." PNEUMA renders "marching out boldly" -- an interpretive paraphrase that loses the Hebrew idiom. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "with a high hand" -- preserving the Hebrew idiom that carries force beyond any single English paraphrase. LXX: *en cheiri hupsēlē* ("with a high hand"), translating literally.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- The Egyptians overtake Israel** -- Hebrew: *vayyirdefu Mitsrayim achareihem vayyassigu otam chonim al-hayyam kol-sus rekhev Par'oh ufarashav vecheilo al-Pi haChirot lifnei Ba'al Tsefon*. The verb *vayyassigu* (Hiphil of *nasag*, "to reach, overtake") -- the Egyptian army catches Israel at the precise location YHWH commanded. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for the military catalog.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Hiqriv* and *vayyits'aqu*: "Pharaoh drew near" and "they cried out"** -- Hebrew: *uFar'oh hiqriv vayis'u venei-Yisra'el et-eineihem vehinneh Mitsrayim nosea achareihem vayyire'u me'od vayyits'aqu venei-Yisra'el el-YHWH*. The verb *hiqriv* (Hiphil of *qarav*, "to come near") is Pharaoh's aggressive approach. The singular participle *nosea* ("marching") -- Egypt as a collective singular mass bearing down. The verb *vayyits'aqu* ("they cried out," from *tsa'aq*) is the same verb used for the cry of slavery in 2:23, now the cry of terror. EMET renders "Par'oh drew near [hiqriv]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Pharaoh drew near." PNEUMA renders "As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up and saw the Egyptians bearing down on them." The consensus follows LOGOS: "Pharaoh drew near" -- preserving the Hebrew's abrupt, dramatic syntax. PNEUMA's "bearing down" is vivid but interpretive. The word "behold" (*hinneh*) is preserved in the consensus as a marker of sudden visual shock.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Hamibli ein-qevarim*: "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt?" -- the great sarcasm** -- Hebrew: *vayomru el-Mosheh hamibli ein-qevarim beMitsrayim leqachtanu lamut bammidbar mah-zot asita lanu lehotsi'anu miMitsrayim*. The construction *hamibli ein-qevarim* ("is it from the lack of there being no graves?") piles negation upon negation in devastating sarcasm. Egypt -- the land of pyramids, tombs, and mortuary temples -- surely has graves enough. The irony is bitter, multi-layered, and one of the most memorable lines in the Hebrew Bible. All three translators preserve the sarcasm. EMET renders "Is it because there are no qevarim [graves] in Mitsrayim" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt." PNEUMA renders "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt." The consensus follows LOGOS: "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt" -- preserving the present-tense directness of the Hebrew sarcasm (PNEUMA's past tense "were there" slightly dulls the immediacy). LXX: *para to mē huparchein mnēmata en gē Aiguptō* ("because there are no tombs in the land of Egypt?").

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Chadal mimmenu*: "leave us alone" -- the slave mentality** -- Hebrew: *halo-zeh haddavar asher dibbarnnu elekha beMitsrayim lemor chadal mimmenu vena'avdah et-Mitsrayim ki tov lanu avod et-Mitsrayim mimmutenu bammidbar*. The verb *chadal* ("cease, desist") -- "stop interfering with us." The double use of *avad* ("to serve") exposes the tragedy: the people prefer the servitude they know to the freedom they do not. This is the first full articulation of the wilderness complaint pattern that will dominate chapters 15-17 and Numbers. EMET renders "leave us alone [khadal]." LOGOS renders "leave us alone." PNEUMA renders "Leave us alone and let us serve the Egyptians." All three agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS: "Leave us alone and let us serve Egypt" -- the Hebrew object is *Mitsrayim* (Egypt as a nation), not "the Egyptians" (PNEUMA).

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Hityatsvu ure'u et-yeshu'at YHWH*: "Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH" -- Moses' greatest command** -- Hebrew: *al-tira'u hityatsvu ure'u et-yeshu'at YHWH asher-ya'aseh lakhem hayyom ki asher re'item et-Mitsrayim hayyom lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam*. Three imperatives define Israel's posture: (1) *al-tira'u* ("do not fear") -- the prohibition against panic; (2) *hityatsvu* (Hithpael of *yatsav*, "to station oneself") -- "stand firm," plant your feet; (3) *ure'u* ("and see") -- open your eyes to what God is about to do. The noun *yeshu'ah* ("salvation, deliverance," from *yasha'*, "to save") is the verbal noun from the root that gives us Yehoshua/Yeshua. This is its first occurrence in the Torah -- the first time divine "salvation" is named. The promise *lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam* ("you will not again see them, unto eternity") is absolute and permanent. EMET renders "stand firm [hityatsavu] and see [u-re'u] the yeshu'at [salvation of] YHWH" with extensive transliteration. LOGOS renders "stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD." PNEUMA renders "stand firm and watch -- you are about to see the deliverance that YHWH will accomplish for you today." The consensus renders "stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH" -- following EMET/LOGOS. "Salvation" (not PNEUMA's "deliverance") preserves the noun *yeshu'ah* and its theological weight as a named concept. "Stand firm" (LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's transliterated form. The phrase "unto eternity" (LOGOS) is preserved for *ad-olam* rather than PNEUMA's omission. LXX: *stēte kai horate tēn sōtērian tēn para tou theou* ("stand and see the salvation from God"), substituting *tou theou* for YHWH.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun*: "YHWH will fight for you, and you shall be silent" -- divine warfare and human silence** -- Hebrew: *YHWH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun*. The verb *yillachem* (Niphal of *lacham*, "to fight") -- YHWH himself enters combat; the battle is his. The verb *tacharishun* (Hiphil of *charash*, "to be silent, hold one's peace") -- the archaic long form with final *-un* -- commands total silence. Israel's complaints (vv. 11-12) and cries (v. 10) must cease. The theological statement is maximally compressed: divine warfare requires human passivity. God fights; the people are silent. This is the quintessential holy war theology. EMET renders "YHWH will fight [yillakhem] for you, and you -- you shall be silent [takharishun]." LOGOS renders "The LORD will fight for you, and you shall be silent." PNEUMA renders "YHWH will fight for you. You need only be still." The consensus renders "YHWH will fight for you, and you shall be silent" -- following EMET/LOGOS. "Be silent" (*tacharishun*) is the precise meaning of the Hiphil of *charash*: cessation of speech, not merely physical stillness (PNEUMA's "be still" shifts toward *damam* territory). The "and" conjunction preserves the Hebrew's *ve-* that binds divine action to human response. LXX: *kurios polemēsei peri humōn, kai humeis sigēsete* ("the Lord will fight for you, and you shall be silent").

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Mah-tits'aq elai*: "Why do you cry out to me?" -- the divine rebuke** -- Hebrew: *mah-tits'aq elai dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyissa'u*. YHWH's response to Moses is abrupt: the time for prayer has passed; now is the time for action. The verb *veyissa'u* (from *nasa'*, "to pull up stakes, journey") commands forward movement -- into the sea. The tension between Moses' "stand still" (v. 13) and YHWH's "journey on" (v. 15) is deliberate: there is a time for watching and a time for walking. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Uveqa'ehu*: "and divide it" -- the splitting command** -- Hebrew: *ve'attah harem et-mattekha uneteh et-yadkha al-hayyam uveqa'ehu veyavo'u venei-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabbashah*. Three imperatives to Moses: *harem* ("lift up," Hiphil of *rum*), *uneteh* ("stretch out," Qal of *natah*), *uveqa'ehu* ("divide it," Qal of *baqa'* with 3ms suffix). The verb *baqa'* ("to split, cleave, break open") is a word of violent rupture -- used for splitting wood (Eccl. 10:9), splitting rocks for water (Ps. 78:15), and rending the earth (Num. 16:31). The sea is not gently parted; it is torn open. The *matteh* ("staff") is the rod that turned to a serpent, struck the Nile, and extended over Egypt during the plagues. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *rēxon autēn* ("tear it apart"), using *rhēgnumi* -- a more violent term.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Hineni mechazzeq*: "behold, I am strengthening" -- the third hardening** -- Hebrew: *va'ani hineni mechazzeq et-lev Mitsrayim veyavo'u achareihem ve'ikkavdah bePar'oh uvkhol-cheilo berikhbo uvfarashav*. The construction *hineni* + participle (*mechazzeq*, Piel participle of *chazaq*) indicates imminent, ongoing action: "I am [in the process of] strengthening." This is the third and final *chazaq* Piel in the chapter, and uniquely it extends the hardening beyond Pharaoh to "the heart of the Egyptians" -- the entire nation's will is now under divine compulsion. The repeated *ve'ikkavdah* from v. 4 confirms the purpose: glory through judgment. EMET renders "I [am] making strong [mekhazeq]." LOGOS renders "I am strengthening." PNEUMA renders "I am about to harden." The consensus follows LOGOS: "I am strengthening" -- preserving the Piel participle's present progressive sense and the *chazaq* root consistency. LXX: *sklērunō tēn kardian Pharaō kai tōn Aiguptiōn pantōn*.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Behikkavdi*: "when I am glorified" -- the knowledge formula completed** -- Hebrew: *veyed'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH behikkavdi bePar'oh berikhbo uvfarashav*. The Niphal infinitive construct *behikkavdi* ("when I am glorified / when I get glory") completes the *k-v-d* arc: Pharaoh's heavy heart becomes God's heavy glory. The knowledge formula recurs from v. 4: Egypt will *know* YHWH. The exodus is not private salvation but public revelation. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Mal'akh ha'Elohim*: "the angel of God" -- the guide becomes the guard** -- Hebrew: *vayyissa mal'akh ha'Elohim haholekh lifnei machaneh Yisra'el vayyeylekh me'acharehem vayyissa ammud he'anan mippneihem vayyaammod me'acharehem*. The *mal'akh ha'Elohim* ("angel/messenger of God") is distinguished from but closely identified with the pillar of cloud -- both move simultaneously from front to rear. The participle *haholekh* ("the one going") links this angel to YHWH's going (*holekh*) before them in 13:21. The relationship between YHWH and his *mal'akh* is fluid in the Pentateuch (cf. Gen. 16:7-13; Exod. 3:2-4). The theological shift is profound: the God who leads from the front now shields from behind. EMET renders "the mal'akh [angel/messenger] of ha-Elohim [God]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the angel of God." PNEUMA renders "the angel of God." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "the angel of God" -- the standard English rendering of *mal'akh ha'Elohim*. "Angel" rather than "messenger" (both valid for *mal'akh*) follows established English convention for divine envoys. LXX: *ho angelos tou theou* ("the angel of God").

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Vayya'er et-hallaylah*: "it lit up the night" -- simultaneous darkness and light** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo bein machaneh Mitsrayim uvein machaneh Yisra'el vayehi he'anan vehachoshekh vayya'er et-hallaylah velo-qarav zeh el-zeh kol-hallaylah*. This is one of the most difficult clauses in the chapter. The Hebrew reads literally: "and-it-was the-cloud and-the-darkness and-it-lit-up the-night." The cloud-and-darkness faced Egypt (keeping them in darkness); the fire side illuminated the night for Israel. The same divine presence produces opposite effects depending on one's relation to the God behind the cloud -- an echo of the ninth plague (10:21-23). All three translators follow the MT. EMET renders "it illuminated [vaya'er] the lailah [night]." LOGOS renders "it lit up the night." PNEUMA paraphrases: "The cloud brought darkness to one side and light to the other." The consensus follows LOGOS: "it lit up the night" -- preserving the MT's terse, enigmatic phrasing rather than PNEUMA's explanatory expansion. The difficulty of the Hebrew should be felt by the reader. LXX reads *kai diēlthen hē nux* ("and the night passed"), a significantly different reading that flattens the paradox.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Beruach qadim azzah*: "by a strong east wind" -- the natural instrument of the supernatural act** -- Hebrew: *vayyeyt Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyolekh YHWH et-hayyam beruach qadim azzah kol-hallaylah vayyasem et-hayyam lecharavah vayyibbaq'u hammayim*. The divine agency operates through natural means: YHWH "caused the sea to go" (*vayyolekh*, Hiphil of *halakh*) by means of a *ruach qadim azzah* ("a strong east wind"). The noun *ruach* means both "wind" and "spirit" -- the same word that hovered over the waters in Gen. 1:2. The adjective *qadim* ("eastern") indicates the desert wind, the sirocco. The adjective *azzah* ("strong, fierce") agrees with the feminine *ruach*. Three result clauses: YHWH caused the sea to move, he made it into dry land (*lecharavah*), and the waters were divided (*vayyibbaq'u*, Niphal of *baqa'*). The two terms for dry ground -- *charavah* (v. 21, dryness from water withdrawal) and *yabbashah* (v. 22, solid dry earth) -- together emphasize the completeness of the transformation. EMET renders "a ruach qadim azzah [strong east wind]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "a strong east wind." PNEUMA renders "a powerful east wind." The consensus follows LOGOS: "a strong east wind" -- preserving the Hebrew's *azzah* ("strong") precisely. "Powerful" (PNEUMA) is interpretively valid but less exact. LXX reads *en anemō notō biaiō* ("by a violent south wind"), a significant geographical variant reading *qadim* as south rather than east.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Chomah*: "wall" -- the waters as architecture** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo'u venei-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabbashah vehamayim lahem chomah miminam umissemolam*. The noun *chomah* ("wall") -- the same word used for a city wall (Josh. 2:15) -- portrays the waters as solid, vertical, architectural barriers. This is not heaped water but walls. The image transcends naturalistic explanation: water has become structure. The phrase recurs verbatim in v. 29, forming an inclusio around the crossing-and-destruction narrative. Some manuscripts read *chamah* ("wrath, heat") instead of *chomah* ("wall"), but the standard Masoretic reading is strongly supported. All three translators render "wall" for *chomah*. The consensus follows the unanimous rendering. LXX: *to hudōr autois teichos ek dexiōn kai teichos ex euōnumōn* ("the water was to them a wall on the right and a wall on the left"), doubling the noun for emphasis.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- The Egyptians enter the sea** -- Hebrew: *vayyirdefu Mitsrayim vayyavo'u achareihem kol sus Par'oh rikhbo ufarashav el-tokh hayyam*. The Egyptian army follows Israel into the corridor between the walls of water -- the trap is sprung. They enter voluntarily, driven by their hardened hearts. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Be'ashmoret habboqer vayyashqef YHWH*: "in the morning watch YHWH looked down" -- the divine gaze** -- Hebrew: *vayehi be'ashmoret habboqer vayyashqef YHWH el-machaneh Mitsrayim be'ammud esh va'anan vayyahom et machaneh Mitsrayim*. The *ashmoret habboqer* ("morning watch") is the last of the three night watches, roughly 2:00-6:00 AM -- the darkest hour before dawn. The verb *vayyashqef* (Hiphil of *shaqaf*, "to look down from above, to gaze down") implies a downward gaze from an elevated position -- God peers through the pillar as through a weapon-sight. The verb is used of God looking down from heaven (Ps. 14:2; 102:20) and precedes judgment: when YHWH *shaqaf*, destruction follows (cf. Gen. 19:28). The verb *vayyahom* (from *hamam*, "to throw into confusion, to rout") is the characteristic holy war verb (Josh. 10:10; Judg. 4:15; 1 Sam. 7:10) -- divine panic that shatters military cohesion. EMET renders "YHWH looked down [vayashqef]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the LORD looked down." PNEUMA renders "YHWH looked down." All three agree on "looked down." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "YHWH looked down" -- the Hiphil of *shaqaf* is accurately rendered as a downward gaze. "Threw into confusion" (LOGOS) is adopted over "confused" (EMET, slightly weak) and "threw them into a panic" (PNEUMA, slightly interpretive). LXX: *epeblepsen kurios epi tēn parembolēn tōn Aiguptiōn* ("the Lord looked upon the camp of the Egyptians").

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Vayyasar et-ofan markevotav*: "he removed the wheels of their chariots" -- and *bikhvedut*: "with heaviness"** -- Hebrew: *vayyasar et ofan markevotav vaynahagehu bikhvedut vayyomer Mitsrayim anusah mippnei Yisra'el ki YHWH nilcham lahem beMitsrayim*. **Key textual decision:** The verb *vayyasar* (from *sur*, "to turn aside, remove") in the MT indicates God caused the chariot wheels to come off. The LXX reads *sunedēsen* ("he bound together"), and some manuscripts and the Samaritan Pentateuch support a variant reading *vayyasset/vayyesor* ("he bound/locked"), suggesting the wheels were clogged or locked rather than removed. The consensus follows the MT Kethiv *vayyasar* -- "he removed" -- as the primary reading. The noun *bikhvedut* ("with heaviness") from the root *k-v-d* is devastatingly ironic: the same "heaviness" that defined Pharaoh's stubborn heart now physically impedes his chariots. The metaphor has become literal. Egypt's cry *anusah mippnei Yisra'el* ("let me flee from Israel") reverses the entire power dynamic -- the superpower flees the slave nation. Their recognition *ki YHWH nilcham lahem* ("for YHWH fights for them") echoes Moses' exact words from v. 14: what Moses declared as prophecy, the Egyptians confess as experience. EMET renders "He removed [vayasar] the wheel [ofan]." LOGOS renders "He turned aside the wheels." PNEUMA renders "He jammed the wheels." The consensus renders "he removed the wheels" -- following EMET and the primary sense of *sur* in the Hiphil ("to cause to turn aside, to remove"). LOGOS's "turned aside" is too ambiguous; PNEUMA's "jammed" conflates the MT with the LXX variant. The LXX variant *sunedēsen* ("bound") is noted as a significant alternative tradition. LXX: *sunedēsen tous axonas tōn harmatōn autōn* ("he bound the axles of their chariots").

> **[fn26] v.26 -- The command for the waters to return** -- Hebrew: *neteh et-yadkha al-hayyam veyashuvu hammayim al-Mitsrayim al-rikhbo ve'al-parashav*. The verb *veyashuvu* ("shall return," from *shuv*) -- the same verb used in v. 2 for Israel's turning back now describes the waters' return. All three translators agree. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Le'etano* and *vayyena'er*: "to its normal flow" and "YHWH shook off" -- the sea restored and Egypt discarded** -- Hebrew: *vayyeyt Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyashov hayyam lifnot boqer le'etano uMitsrayim nassim liqra'to vayyena'er YHWH et-Mitsrayim betokh hayyam*. The phrase *lifnot boqer* ("at the turning of the morning") marks dawn's approach -- the same dawn that began with the morning watch (v. 24). The term *le'etano* (from *eitan*, "perennial, enduring, strong") means "to its normal flow, to its perpetual state" -- the sea returns to what it always was; the miraculous interruption was temporary. The verb *vayyena'er* (Piel of *na'ar*, "to shake off, shake out") is strikingly physical: YHWH "shook off" the Egyptians into the sea as one shakes insects from a garment or dust from a cloth. The military superpower of the ancient world is flicked into the sea like debris. EMET renders "to its steady-flow [le-eitano]" and "YHWH shook off [vaye'na'er]." LOGOS renders "to its normal flow" and "the LORD shook off." PNEUMA renders "to its normal course" and "YHWH swept them." The consensus renders "to its normal flow" (following LOGOS -- "flow" captures *eitan*'s sense of perpetual water movement better than EMET's "steady-flow" or PNEUMA's "course") and "YHWH shook off" (following EMET/LOGOS -- "shook off" preserves the visceral physical image of *na'ar*; PNEUMA's "swept" loses the specific verb). LXX: *eis ton archaion topon* ("to its former place") for *le'etano*; *exetinaxen kurios tous Aiguptious* ("the Lord shook out the Egyptians") for *vayyena'er*.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad*: "not even one remained"** -- Hebrew: *vayyashuvu hammayim vayyekhassu et-harekhev ve'et-happarashim lekhol cheil Par'oh habba'im achareihem bayyam lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad*. The phrase *lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad* ("there did not remain among them even one") is the language of total annihilation. The verb *nish'ar* (Niphal of *sha'ar*, "to remain, be left over") is negated absolutely: no remnant, no survivor. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn29] v.29 -- The inclusio with v. 22** -- Hebrew: *uvenei Yisra'el halekhu bayyabbashah betokh hayyam vehamayim lahem chomah miminam umissemolam*. This verse nearly repeats v. 22 but with a crucial shift: in v. 22 they "went into" (*vayyavo'u*) the sea; here they "walked" (*halekhu*) through it -- the passage is complete. The repetition frames the Egyptian destruction (vv. 23-28) between two statements of Israel's safe passage, creating a literary envelope: the same water, two opposite fates. All three translators agree. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Vayyosha YHWH*: "YHWH saved Israel" -- salvation fulfilled** -- Hebrew: *vayyosha YHWH bayyom hahu et-Yisra'el miyyad Mitsrayim vayyar Yisra'el et-Mitsrayim met al-sefat hayyam*. The verb *vayyosha* (Hiphil of *yasha'*, "to save") is the verbal fulfillment of the noun *yeshu'ah* ("salvation") from v. 13: what Moses promised, YHWH has accomplished. The phrase *miyyad Mitsrayim* ("from the hand of Egypt") completes the *yad* ("hand") arc: Israel's high hand (v. 8), Moses' outstretched hand (vv. 21, 27), and now deliverance from Egypt's hand. The singular *met* ("dead") used collectively is more devastating than a plural: Egypt is dead. The *sefat hayyam* ("shore/lip of the sea") -- the bodies wash up as visible proof. EMET renders "YHWH saved [vayosha]." LOGOS renders "the LORD saved." PNEUMA renders "YHWH delivered." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "YHWH saved" -- preserving the *yasha'* root that connects to *yeshu'ah* in v. 13. PNEUMA's "delivered" is valid but loses the verbal echo. LXX: *errusato kurios ton Israēl* ("the Lord rescued Israel").

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Vayyar... vayyir'u... vayya'aminu*: the sight-fear-faith sequence -- the theological climax** -- Hebrew: *vayyar Yisra'el et-hayyad haggedolah asher asah YHWH beMitsrayim vayyir'u ha'am et-YHWH vayya'aminu baYHWH uvMosheh avdo*. Three verbs in climactic sequence: (1) *vayyar* ("they saw") -- the physical evidence of the great hand; (2) *vayyir'u* ("they feared") -- reverential awe replacing the terror of v. 10; (3) *vayya'aminu* (Hiphil of *aman*, "they believed, they trusted") -- covenantal trust arising from witnessed events. The progression is from sight to fear to faith -- the Hebrew Bible's foundational model of *emunah*: faith is not blind; it is the response of those who have seen what God does. The *hayyad haggedolah* ("the great hand") completes the progression from "strong hand" (*yad chazaqah*, 3:19; 6:1; 13:3) to "high hand" (*yad ramah*, v. 8) to "great hand" -- the trajectory of divine power from oppression through liberation to consummation. The verb *he'emin* (Hiphil of *aman*) is a covenantal term: not intellectual assent but relational trust -- placing confidence in another's reliability. The root *aman* gives us "amen" and *emunah* ("faithfulness"). The addition *uvMosheh avdo* ("and in Moses his servant") establishes Moses' authority on the basis of the sea crossing -- the title *eved* ("servant") is one of honor (cf. Abraham, Gen. 26:24; David, 2 Sam. 7:5; the Suffering Servant, Isa. 52:13). EMET renders "they believed [vaya'aminu] in YHWH." LOGOS renders "they believed in the LORD." PNEUMA renders "they put their trust in YHWH." All three agree on substance. The consensus renders "they believed in YHWH" -- following EMET/LOGOS. "Believed" preserves the Hiphil of *aman* as a cognitive-relational act; PNEUMA's "put their trust" is valid but adds words not in the Hebrew. LXX: *episteusen ho laos tō theō kai Mōusē tō theraponti autou* ("the people believed God and Moses his attendant"), using *therapōn* ("attendant, minister") rather than *doulos* ("slave") for *eved* -- a term the LXX reserves almost exclusively for Moses.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. The Hardening Cluster: *chazaq* Piel (vv. 4, 8, 17)

| Translator | v.4 Rendering | v.8 Rendering | v.17 Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will make strong" | "YHWH made strong" | "I [am] making strong" |
| LOGOS | "I will strengthen" | "the LORD strengthened" | "I am strengthening" |
| PNEUMA | "I will harden" | "YHWH hardened" | "I am about to harden" |

The consensus renders "strengthen" for all three *chazaq* Piel occurrences, following the convention established in earlier chapters. All three use the same Hebrew root (*chazaq*) in the same stem (Piel) with the same agent (YHWH). The consistency of rendering preserves the literary and theological pattern: God's announced purpose (v. 4) is narrated as fact (v. 8) and reiterated as ongoing action (v. 17). PNEUMA's "harden" is the more conventional English rendering but obscures the Hebrew root distinction between *chazaq* ("strengthen"), *kaved* ("make heavy"), and *qashah* ("make hard"). The three-root system is theologically significant and should be preserved in translation.

### 2. *Ve'ikkavdah* (vv. 4, 17): "I will be glorified"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will get glory" | Active: God acquires glory |
| LOGOS | "I will be glorified" | Passive: God is glorified |
| PNEUMA | "I will display my glory" | Active: God shows glory |

The consensus renders "I will be glorified" -- following LOGOS. The Niphal of *kaved* is best understood as a tolerative/reflexive passive: "I will let myself be glorified" or "I will be shown as glorious." EMET's "get glory" is too transactional; PNEUMA's "display my glory" adds an active verb not in the Hebrew. "I will be glorified" preserves the *k-v-d* root connection to the hardening verb (*kaved lev*, "heavy heart") while honoring the Niphal stem.

### 3. *Beyad ramah* (v. 8): "with a high hand"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "be-yad ramah [with a high/uplifted hand]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "with a high hand" | Formal: preserves idiom |
| PNEUMA | "marching out boldly" | Dynamic: interprets idiom |

The consensus renders "with a high hand" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew idiom carries semantic richness (defiance, boldness, triumph, liberation) that no single English interpretation can capture. PNEUMA's "boldly" extracts one dimension and discards the rest. Preserving the idiom allows the reader to encounter its strangeness and its power.

### 4. Sarcasm Preservation (v. 11): "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt?"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Is it because there are no qevarim [graves] in Mitsrayim" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt" | Formal: preserves sarcasm |
| PNEUMA | "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt" | Dynamic: past tense |

All three translators preserve the sarcasm effectively. The consensus follows LOGOS with present tense ("are") rather than PNEUMA's past tense ("were"), preserving the immediacy of the Hebrew's direct speech. The triple negative of the Hebrew (*hamibli ein*) is necessarily simplified in English, but the sarcastic force is maintained.

### 5. *Yeshu'at YHWH* (v. 13): "the salvation of YHWH"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the yeshu'at [salvation of] YHWH" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "the salvation of the LORD" | Formal: standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the deliverance that YHWH will accomplish" | Dynamic: expanded |

The consensus renders "the salvation of YHWH." "Salvation" (not "deliverance") preserves the nominal form *yeshu'ah* and its theological weight as a named concept -- this is the first occurrence in the Torah. "Deliverance" (PNEUMA) is a valid synonym but loses the verbal echo with *vayyosha* ("saved") in v. 30.

### 6. *Tacharishun* (v. 14): "you shall be silent"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall be silent [takharishun]" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "you shall be silent" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "You need only be still" | Dynamic |

The consensus renders "you shall be silent" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The Hiphil of *charash* specifically denotes cessation of speech, not physical stillness (*damam*). PNEUMA's "be still" shifts the meaning toward bodily immobility. After Israel's verbal complaints in vv. 11-12, the command to *silence* is precisely targeted.

### 7. *Mal'akh ha'Elohim* (v. 19): "the angel of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the mal'akh [angel/messenger] of ha-Elohim [God]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "the angel of God" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the angel of God" | Dynamic |

All three agree on "angel of God." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Angel" is retained over "messenger" per convention for divine envoys.

### 8. *Ruach qadim azzah* (v. 21): "a strong east wind"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a ruach qadim azzah [strong east wind]" | Literal with transliteration |
| LOGOS | "a strong east wind" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "a powerful east wind" | Dynamic |

The consensus follows LOGOS: "a strong east wind." The adjective *azzah* ("strong, fierce") is precisely rendered. PNEUMA's "powerful" is slightly more interpretive.

### 9. *Chomah* (vv. 22, 29): "wall"

All three translators agree: "wall." No disagreement. The literal preservation of this architectural metaphor is essential -- water as vertical structure, not mere accumulation.

### 10. *Vayyashqef* (v. 24): "YHWH looked down"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "YHWH looked down [vayashqef]" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the LORD looked down" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH looked down" | Dynamic |

All three agree: "looked down." The Hiphil of *shaqaf* specifically denotes a downward gaze from an elevated position. No disagreement.

### 11. *Vayyasar et-ofan* (v. 25): "he removed the wheels" (MT) vs. "he bound/clogged" (LXX)

| Translator | Rendering | Textual Base |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He removed [vayasar] the wheel [ofan]" | MT (Kethiv) |
| LOGOS | "He turned aside the wheels" | MT |
| PNEUMA | "He jammed the wheels" | Conflation (MT + LXX) |

The consensus renders "he removed the wheels" -- following EMET and the primary Hiphil sense of *sur*. This is a significant textual decision. The MT reads *vayyasar* ("he removed/turned aside"), while the LXX reads *sunedēsen* ("he bound together"), suggesting a variant Vorlage. LOGOS's "turned aside" is etymologically defensible but contextually unclear. PNEUMA's "jammed" conflates both traditions. The consensus follows the MT while noting the LXX variant as a legitimate alternative tradition.

### 12. *Vayyena'er* (v. 27): "YHWH shook off"

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "YHWH shook off [vaye'na'er]" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the LORD shook off" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH swept them" | Dynamic |

The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "YHWH shook off." The Piel of *na'ar* means to shake off/shake out -- like shaking insects from a garment. The image reduces Egypt's military might to something casually discarded. PNEUMA's "swept" loses this specific physical action.

### 13. *Vayya'aminu* (v. 31): "they believed in" -- the sight-fear-faith sequence

| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they believed [vaya'aminu] in YHWH" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "they believed in the LORD" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "they put their trust in YHWH" | Dynamic |

The consensus renders "they believed in YHWH." The Hiphil of *aman* is a single verb in Hebrew; "put their trust" (PNEUMA) expands it to a phrase. "Believed" captures the cognitive-relational dimension of *he'emin* while maintaining verbal economy.

### 14. Recurring Convention Disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (He, His); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*. (Recurring, LOW)

**d. Hebrew names:** EMET uses transliterated forms (Mosheh, Par'oh, Mitsrayim); LOGOS and PNEUMA use standard English forms (Moses, Pharaoh, Egypt). SOPHIA follows standard English forms per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**e. Transliteration in-text:** EMET embeds Hebrew transliterations throughout; LOGOS brackets them; PNEUMA uses English only. SOPHIA follows English text with Hebrew in footnotes. (Recurring, LOW)

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 14

| Verse | Hebrew | Verb/Root | Form | Agent | SOPHIA Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 14:4 | *vechizzaqti et-lev Par'oh* | *chazaq* | Piel (1cs perfect + vav consec.) | God ("I will strengthen") | "I will strengthen Pharaoh's heart" |
| 14:8 | *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitsrayim* | *chazaq* | Piel (3ms imperfect + vav consec.) | God (YHWH strengthened) | "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" |
| 14:17 | *va'ani hineni mechazzeq et-lev Mitsrayim* | *chazaq* | Piel (ms participle) | God ("I am strengthening") | "I am strengthening the heart of the Egyptians" |

*Note: All three hardening verbs in Exodus 14 use the same root chazaq in the Piel stem, and all three have God as the explicit agent. This is the final cluster of hardening verbs in the Exodus narrative, and it is theologically decisive: at the Sea of Reeds, there is no ambiguity about agency. God himself strengthens Pharaoh's heart (v. 4, announced; v. 8, narrated) and the hearts of all the Egyptians (v. 17, announced). The Piel form is causative -- God actively causes the hardening. The purpose is stated twice: ve'ikkavdah ("and I will be glorified," vv. 4, 17). The root k-v-d ("heavy/glorious") that described Pharaoh's stubborn heart (kaved, 7:14; 8:15, 28; 9:7, 34) now describes God's glory manifest through Pharaoh's destruction. The heaviness that was Pharaoh's sin becomes the glory that is God's vindication. After 14:17, the hardening verb sequence that began at 4:21 is complete. The progression across Exodus 4-14 shows roughly equal distribution between divine agency and Pharaoh's self-hardening in chapters 4-11, resolving into exclusively divine agency in chapter 14.*

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 14:1-31 (31 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant (especially the south wind / east wind divergence at v. 21, the bound/removed chariot wheels at v. 25, the darkness-only reading at v. 20, and the therapōn/eved distinction at v. 31). Three hardening verbs in chapter 14: all chazaq Piel, all divine agent (vv. 4, 8, 17). Key renderings: "I will strengthen... I will be glorified" (v. 4, the hardening-glory nexus), "with a high hand" (v. 8, the idiom of liberation), "Is it because there are no graves in Egypt" (v. 11, sarcasm preserved), "Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH" (v. 13, first yeshu'ah in the Torah), "you shall be silent" (v. 14, holy silence), "the angel of God" (v. 19, mal'akh ha'Elohim), "a strong east wind" (v. 21, ruach qadim azzah), "wall" (vv. 22/29, chomah -- architectural water), "YHWH looked down" (v. 24, the divine gaze), "he removed the wheels" (v. 25, MT reading), "shook off" (v. 27, vayyena'er), "to its normal flow" (v. 27, le'etano), "they believed in YHWH" (v. 31, the sight-fear-faith climax). The thirteen key translation decisions identified in the prompt have been adjudicated with full documentation.*

--- SOPHIA
